subject: Physics
title: The Discovery of Radioactivity
description: Learn The Discovery of Radioactivity in one minute.

hook.text: In 1896, Henri Becquerel left uranium salts on a photographic plate in a dark drawer. The plate was fogged—by invisible rays.

buildup.text: Becquerel was studying phosphorescence—materials that glow after absorbing sunlight.

discovery.text: On a cloudy day, he stored his materials in a drawer. The plate developed an image anyway—no sunlight needed.

twist.text: The uranium was emitting penetrating rays all by itself. Becquerel had discovered radioactivity by accident.

climax.text: Marie Curie later coined the term 'radioactivity' and pursued the discovery to its full consequences.

punchline.text: A cloudy day and a dark drawer. Radioactivity discovered by lucky accident.

quiz.question: How was radioactivity discovered?
options: Uranium fogged a photographic plate stored in a dark drawer without sunlight
Marie Curie deliberately exposed plates to radium
X-rays were found to make uranium glow
